title: Naked Bible Podcast Episode 94: The Sin of the Watchers and Galatians 3-4
author: heiser

entries.title: The Watchers' Sin as the Origin of Evil — Not Adam's Fall Alone
entries.content_summary: Paul says the Law was 'added because of transgressions' (Galatians 3:19). The common assumption is that this refers to Adam's sin. But nearly every Second Temple Jewish text that comments on human depravity attributes the proliferation of evil not to Adam but to the sin of the Watchers in Genesis 6:1-4. The Watchers (divine beings who transgressed by taking human wives) produced the Nephilim, whose deaths released disembodied spirits — called 'bastard spirits,' 'Watchers,' and 'demons' in Enochian literature. Some Second Temple writers considered the Watchers' transgression more significant than Adam's disobedience as the origin of widespread human evil. Paul, writing in this intellectual milieu, may have had the Watchers tradition in view when he wrote about the Law being added 'because of transgressions.'
entries.content_detail: Tyler Stewart's SBL paper ('Fallen Angels, Bastard Spirits, and the Birth of God's Son: An Enochic Etiology of Evil in Galatians 3:19-4:11') argued Paul's discussion of the Law's purpose reflects the Watchers tradition. The Watchers taught forbidden knowledge (metalwork for weapons, cosmetics, sorcery — 1 Enoch 8:1-3) that corrupted humanity. Irenaeus and other early church fathers recognized the Watchers tradition as lurking behind discussions of human depravity. The 'elements of the world' (stoicheia tou kosmou) in Galatians 4:3,9 may refer to the rebellious divine beings (the Watchers/gods of the nations) under whose authority the Gentile nations were placed at Babel (Deut 32:8-9).

entries.title: Demons as Disembodied Spirits of Dead Nephilim — The Second Temple Understanding
entries.content_summary: In Second Temple Judaism (1 Enoch, Jubilees, Book of Giants), the Nephilim were the hybrid offspring of the Watchers (divine beings) and human women. When the Nephilim died (in the Flood and afterward), their spirits became disembodied — these are the demons (shedim) of the biblical text. They are called 'bastard spirits' because they are neither fully divine nor fully human. This understanding distinguishes demons from the fallen divine council members (the gods of the nations in Deuteronomy 32) and from Satan/the nachash. There are thus THREE categories of evil spiritual beings in the biblical framework: (1) the nachash/Satan — the original rebel from Eden; (2) the rebellious gods of the nations — divine council members who became corrupt (Psalm 82); (3) demons — disembodied Nephilim spirits.
entries.content_detail: The term 'Watchers' (irin in Aramaic) comes from Daniel 4:17 where divine council members are called Watchers. In 1 Enoch, the same term is applied to the divine beings who transgressed in Genesis 6. The Watchers are distinct from the gods of the nations (Deut 32:8-9) — the Watchers transgressed by leaving their proper domain (their 'first estate' per Jude 6) to cohabit with humans, while the gods of the nations were legitimately assigned to govern nations but became corrupt in their governance.
